examples.explanation: Start: capacity=5, index=0 (weight=2, value=10). Branch include: capacity=3, index=0, include again: capacity=1, index=0, include invalid (capacity<2), exclude: capacity=1, index=1, include invalid (weight=3>1), exclude: capacity=1, index=2 (end) return 0. Backtrack: value=10+10+0=20. Branch exclude from first include: capacity=3, index=1, include: capacity=0, index=1, include invalid, exclude: capacity=0, index=2 return 0, value=20+0=20. Max of include branches: 20. Backtrack to start exclude: capacity=5, index=1, include: capacity=2, index=1, include invalid, exclude: capacity=2, index=2 return 0, value=20+0=20. Exclude: capacity=5, index=2 return 0. Max=20. But optimal is two of weight2 (value20) and one of weight3 (value20) invalid due to capacity. Actually trace: Start capacity5 index0: include->capacity3 index0: include->capacity1 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity1 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity1 index2 return0, value=10+10+0=20; exclude->capacity3 index1: include->capacity0 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=20+0=20, max=20; exclude->capacity5 index1: include->capacity2 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity2 index2 return0, value=20+0=20; exclude->capacity5 index2 return0; max overall=20. But correct optimal: include weight2 twice (value20) and weight3 once (value20) exceeds capacity5. Actually best: weight2 twice (4 capacity, value20) and cannot add weight3. Or weight3 once (3 capacity, value20) and weight2 once (2 capacity, value10) total capacity5 value30. Trace that: Start capacity5 index0: include weight2->capacity3 index0: include weight2->capacity1 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity1 index1: include weight3 invalid, exclude->capacity1 index2 return0, value=10+10+0=20; exclude->capacity3 index1: include weight3->capacity0 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=10+20+0=30; exclude->capacity5 index1: include weight3->capacity2 index1: include weight2->capacity-1 invalid, exclude->capacity2 index2 return0, value=20+10+0=30; exclude->capacity5 index2 return0. Max=30.

Start capacity4 index0 (weight1 value1): include->capacity3 index0: include->capacity2 index0: include->capacity1 index0: include->capacity0 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index1: include weight2 invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=1+1+1+1+0=4; exclude->capacity1 index1: include weight2 invalid, exclude->capacity1 index2 return0, value=1+1+1+0=3; max branch. Actually full trace: At each step two choices. Optimal: four weight2 items invalid (capacity8), so two weight2 items (capacity4 value10) or four weight1 items (value4). But weight2 items: capacity4 index0 exclude->capacity4 index1 include weight2->capacity2 index1 include weight2->capacity0 index1 include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=5+5+0=10. Compare: include weight1 four times: value4. So max=10. But output should be 10, not 20. Correcting: output=10. Trace: Start capacity4 index0: include weight1->capacity3 index0: include->capacity2 index0: include->capacity1 index0: include->capacity0 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index1 include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=1+1+1+1+0=4; exclude paths lead to weight2 items. Actually to get 10: capacity4 index0 exclude->capacity4 index1 include weight2->capacity2 index1 include weight2->capacity0 index1 include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=5+5+0=10. So max=10.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively try all combinations of items by including each item multiple times (since unbounded) or skipping it, exploring all possibilities to maximize value within the weight capacity. This is the most straightforward but inefficient method, suitable only for understanding the problem's naive solution.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Unbounded Knapsack with Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all combinations of items with unlimited repetitions to maximize value within weight capacity.
ladders.explanation: The unbounded knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding a weight capacity, where each item can be used multiple times. The brute force recursion approach systematically explores all possible combinations by making two recursive choices at each step: either include the current item (reducing remaining capacity by its weight and adding its value) or skip it (moving to the next item). This generates a complete decision tree where each leaf represents a valid combination. The intuition is that by exploring all possibilities, we guarantee finding the optimal solution, though this is computationally expensive. The algorithm starts with the full capacity and all items available, recursively branching until capacity is exhausted or all items are considered. The base cases are when capacity becomes negative (invalid combination) or when no items remain (return 0). The recursive relation is: max(value + recursive_call(capacity - weight, same_items), recursive_call(capacity, next_items)). Time complexity is O(2^n) because each item generates two recursive branches, leading to exponential growth. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This approach connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive function that L1's helper supports. Common mistakes include not handling negative capacity correctly (should return negative infinity or large negative to invalidate) and forgetting that items can be reused (same item stays available when included). Real code pattern: def knapsack(capacity, weights, values, index): if capacity < 0: return -inf; if index == len(weights): return 0; include = values[index] + knapsack(capacity-weights[index], weights, values, index) if capacity >= weights[index] else -inf; exclude = knapsack(capacity, weights, values, index+1); return max(include, exclude).

examples.trace: Start: capacity=5, index=0 (weight=2, value=10). Branch include: capacity=3, index=0, include again: capacity=1, index=0, include invalid (capacity<2), exclude: capacity=1, index=1, include invalid (weight=3>1), exclude: capacity=1, index=2 (end) return 0. Backtrack: value=10+10+0=20. Branch exclude from first include: capacity=3, index=1, include: capacity=0, index=1, include invalid, exclude: capacity=0, index=2 return 0, value=20+0=20. Max of include branches: 20. Backtrack to start exclude: capacity=5, index=1, include: capacity=2, index=1, include invalid, exclude: capacity=2, index=2 return 0, value=20+0=20. Exclude: capacity=5, index=2 return 0. Max=20. But optimal is two of weight2 (value20) and one of weight3 (value20) invalid due to capacity. Actually trace: Start capacity5 index0: include->capacity3 index0: include->capacity1 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity1 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity1 index2 return0, value=10+10+0=20; exclude->capacity3 index1: include->capacity0 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=20+0=20, max=20; exclude->capacity5 index1: include->capacity2 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity2 index2 return0, value=20+0=20; exclude->capacity5 index2 return0; max overall=20. But correct optimal: include weight2 twice (value20) and weight3 once (value20) exceeds capacity5. Actually best: weight2 twice (4 capacity, value20) and cannot add weight3. Or weight3 once (3 capacity, value20) and weight2 once (2 capacity, value10) total capacity5 value30. Trace that: Start capacity5 index0: include weight2->capacity3 index0: include weight2->capacity1 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity1 index1: include weight3 invalid, exclude->capacity1 index2 return0, value=10+10+0=20; exclude->capacity3 index1: include weight3->capacity0 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=10+20+0=30; exclude->capacity5 index1: include weight3->capacity2 index1: include weight2->capacity-1 invalid, exclude->capacity2 index2 return0, value=20+10+0=30; exclude->capacity5 index2 return0. Max=30.

Start capacity4 index0 (weight1 value1): include->capacity3 index0: include->capacity2 index0: include->capacity1 index0: include->capacity0 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index1: include weight2 invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=1+1+1+1+0=4; exclude->capacity1 index1: include weight2 invalid, exclude->capacity1 index2 return0, value=1+1+1+0=3; max branch. Actually full trace: At each step two choices. Optimal: four weight2 items invalid (capacity8), so two weight2 items (capacity4 value10) or four weight1 items (value4). But weight2 items: capacity4 index0 exclude->capacity4 index1 include weight2->capacity2 index1 include weight2->capacity0 index1 include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=5+5+0=10. Compare: include weight1 four times: value4. So max=10. But output should be 10, not 20. Correcting: output=10. Trace: Start capacity4 index0: include weight1->capacity3 index0: include->capacity2 index0: include->capacity1 index0: include->capacity0 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index1 include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=1+1+1+1+0=4; exclude paths lead to weight2 items. Actually to get 10: capacity4 index0 exclude->capacity4 index1 include weight2->capacity2 index1 include weight2->capacity0 index1 include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=5+5+0=10. So max=10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Decision Function for One Item
ladders.desc: Helper function that decides whether to include or skip a specific item at a given capacity.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that, given a remaining capacity and a current item index, returns the maximum value achievable by either including the item (if possible) or skipping it. The intuition is that at each decision point, we evaluate two futures: one where we take the item (reducing capacity and adding value, but keeping the item available for reuse) and one where we move to the next item without taking the current one. This encapsulates the greedy-like choice that builds up to the global optimum through recursion. The algorithm: function solve(cap, idx): if cap < 0 return -infinity (invalid state), if idx == n return 0 (no items left). Otherwise, include_val = if cap >= weight[idx] then value[idx] + solve(cap - weight[idx], idx) else -infinity; exclude_val = solve(cap, idx+1); return max(include_val, exclude_val). Time complexity for this function in isolation is O(2^n) as it recursively calls itself, but for a single call it depends on the subtree. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by being the recursive engine that L0 repeatedly calls to explore all combinations. Common mistakes: forgetting to check cap >= weight[idx] before including (leading to negative capacity), and not passing the same idx when including (for unbounded reuse). Real code pattern: def recursive_helper(capacity, index): if capacity < 0: return float('-inf'); if index == len(weights): return 0; include = values[index] + recursive_helper(capacity - weights[index], index) if capacity >= weights[index] else float('-inf'); exclude = recursive_helper(capacity, index + 1); return max(include, exclude).

examples.trace: Call: capacity=3, index=0 (weight2 value10). Check capacity>=2? yes. Include: call capacity=1, index=0: capacity>=2? no, include invalid (-inf), exclude: call capacity=1, index=1: capacity>=3? no, include invalid, exclude: call capacity=1, index=2 return 0. So include path value=10 + max(-inf,0)=10. Exclude: call capacity=3, index=1: capacity>=3? yes, include: call capacity=0, index=1: capacity>=3? no, include invalid, exclude: call capacity=0, index=2 return 0, value=20+0=20; exclude: call capacity=3, index=2 return 0. So exclude path max=20. But overall max(include=10, exclude=20)=20. Wait output for index0 only? Actually sub-routine returns max from this index onward. So output=20. Correcting: For index=0, it considers items from index0 onward, so output=20. Trace: capacity3 index0: include->capacity1 index0: include invalid, exclude->capacity1 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity1 index2 return0, value=10+0=10; exclude->capacity3 index1: include->capacity0 index1: include invalid, exclude->capacity0 index2 return0, value=20+0=20; exclude->capacity3 index2 return0. Max=20.
Call: capacity=2, index=1 (weight3 value10). Check capacity>=3? no, include invalid (-inf). Exclude: call capacity=2, index=2 return 0. Max(-inf,0)=0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Inclusion/Exclusion Decision
ladders.desc: The fundamental recursive choice between taking an item (with reuse) or skipping it.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the recursive decision-making process: for any item at a given capacity, we have two options—include the item or exclude it. If we include, we add its value, reduce the capacity by its weight, and recursively consider the same item again (since unbounded). If we exclude, we move to the next item without changing capacity. The intuition is that by exploring both possibilities at each step, we systematically cover all combinations of items, ensuring that the optimal solution is found in the search space. This is a classic divide-and-conquer strategy where the problem is broken down into smaller subproblems. The algorithm can be expressed as: maxValue(cap, idx) = max(value[idx] + maxValue(cap - weight[idx], idx), maxValue(cap, idx+1)), with base cases for invalid capacity or no items left. Time complexity arises from the binary branching at each item, leading to O(2^n). Space is O(n) due to recursion depth. This logic is the heart of L1's helper function. Common mistakes include not allowing item reuse (incorrectly incrementing idx on include) and not handling the case where inclusion is impossible due to insufficient capacity. Real code pattern: if capacity < 0: return -inf; if idx == n: return 0; include = values[idx] + maxValue(capacity - weights[idx], idx) if capacity >= weights[idx] else -inf; exclude = maxValue(capacity, idx+1); return max(include, exclude).

examples.trace: Assume only one item type with weight2 value5, and after this item, no more items (so exclude leads to 0). Decision: include? capacity4>=2 yes, so include: value5 + decision on capacity2: include? capacity2>=2 yes, value5 + decision on capacity0: include? capacity0>=2 no, so include invalid, exclude leads to 0, so value5+0=5. Total include path: 5+5=10. Exclude: move to next item gives 0. Max(10,0)=10.
Item weight4 > capacity3, so include invalid (-inf). Exclude gives 0. Max(-inf,0)=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Implementing a recursive function that handles base cases for capacity and item index.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on creating a recursive function that properly manages base cases: when the remaining capacity becomes negative, the combination is invalid, so we return a very low value (e.g., negative infinity) to ensure it's not selected; when we've processed all items (index beyond the list), we return 0 as no more value can be added. The intuition is that these base cases terminate the recursion and define the boundaries of valid solutions. The algorithm structure is: function f(cap, idx): if cap < 0: return -infinity; if idx == n: return 0; // recursive cases follow. This ensures that only combinations within capacity are considered, and the recursion stops when no items are left. Time and space analysis are similar to higher levels but focused on the base case handling. This block is essential for L2's core logic to work correctly. Common mistakes: returning 0 for negative capacity (which might incorrectly validate invalid combinations) or not checking index bounds. Real code pattern: def recursive_func(capacity, index): if capacity < 0: return float('-inf'); if index == len(weights): return 0; # proceed with recursion.

examples.trace: Call with capacity=-1: check capacity < 0? yes, return -inf immediately. No further recursion.
Call with index=2, which equals number of items (2). Check index == n? yes, return 0. No item to consider.

testCases.expected: 0 (after recursion, but base case not triggered yet; actually base case for index only, so output depends but for this block, if index not at end, proceed)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Capacity and Item Availability
ladders.desc: The fundamental checks for whether an item can be included based on remaining capacity and if items remain to consider.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two simple checks: first, verifying if the remaining capacity is sufficient to include the current item (i.e., capacity >= weight of item); second, checking if there are more items to process (i.e., current index is within the list bounds). The intuition is that these checks are prerequisites for making the include/exclude decision—they determine feasibility. The algorithm: if capacity >= weight[idx], then inclusion is possible; otherwise, it's not. And if idx < n, items are available; otherwise, we've exhausted all items. Time and space are O(1) for these checks. This operation feeds into L3 by providing the conditions for base cases and recursive calls. Common mistakes: using strict inequality (capacity > weight) instead of >=, which incorrectly excludes cases where capacity exactly equals weight, and off-by-one errors in index checking. Real code pattern: can_include = capacity >= weights[index] if index < len(weights) else False; items_remain = index < len(weights).

examples.trace: Check capacity >= weight: 5 >= 3 is true, so inclusion possible. Check index < n: 0 < 2 is true, so items remain.
Check capacity >= weight: 2 >= 5 is false, so inclusion not possible. Check index < n: 1 < 1 is false (since n=1, index 1 is out of bounds), so no items remain.

testCases.expected: can_include=True (if weight=0, but typically weight>0), assume weight=0 edge: true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: The basic idea of recursion: a function calling itself with smaller inputs, and stopping via base cases.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller instance of the same problem. The foundation involves two key parts: the recursive case, where the function calls itself with modified parameters (e.g., reduced capacity or incremented index), and the base case, which provides a stopping condition to prevent infinite recursion (e.g., when capacity becomes negative or no items are left). The intuition is that complex problems can be broken down into simpler, similar subproblems until they become trivial to solve. For the knapsack problem, recursion allows us to explore all combinations by making decisions at each step. Time and space analysis starts here: each recursive call consumes stack space, and the number of calls can grow exponentially. This concept is prerequisite for L4's checks, as recursion relies on base cases defined by those checks. Common mistakes: missing base cases (leading to infinite recursion), or not reducing problem size in recursive calls (causing no progress). Real code pattern: def recursive_function(param): if base_case_condition: return base_value; else: modified_param = change(param); return combine(param, recursive_function(modified_param)).

examples.input: Function to compute factorial: fact(n) with base case n=0
examples.trace: Call fact(3): n=3 not base case, so return 3 * fact(2). Call fact(2): return 2 * fact(1). Call fact(1): return 1 * fact(0). Call fact(0): base case, return 1. Unwind: fact(1)=1*1=1, fact(2)=2*1=2, fact(3)=3*2=6.

examples.input: Function to sum array: sum_arr(arr, idx) with base case idx=len(arr)
examples.trace: Call sum_arr([1,2,3],0): idx=0 not base case, return arr[0] + sum_arr(arr,1). Call sum_arr(arr,1): return 2 + sum_arr(arr,2). Call sum_arr(arr,2): return 3 + sum_arr(arr,3). Call sum_arr(arr,3): base case idx=3=len, return 0. Unwind: 3+0=3, 2+3=5, 1+5=6.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n*W)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization (top-down DP) to store results of subproblems, where each state represents the maximum value for a given remaining capacity. This avoids redundant calculations by caching results, making it more efficient than brute force while maintaining a recursive structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Unbounded Knapsack with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using top-down DP with memoization to maximize value without exceeding capacity, allowing unlimited item usage.
ladders.explanation: The unbounded knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding capacity W, where each item can be used unlimited times. The intuition is to break the problem into subproblems: for each remaining capacity, decide whether to include an item (and potentially reuse it) or skip it. Memoization stores results of subproblems to avoid redundant calculations. The algorithm uses recursion: base cases are capacity ≤ 0 (return 0) or no items left (return 0). For each item, if its weight ≤ remaining capacity, recursively compute maximum value by either including it (adding its value and reducing capacity by its weight, allowing reuse) or excluding it (moving to next item). The result is cached in a memo dictionary keyed by (index, capacity). Time complexity is O(n*W) due to n items and W capacity states, each computed once. Space is O(n*W) for memo storage plus recursion stack O(W). This feeds into the approach by implementing the complete DP logic. Common mistakes include not handling negative capacities, forgetting to allow item reuse in recursion, or incorrect memo keys. Real code pattern: def knapsack(values, weights, n, W, memo): if W <= 0 or n == 0: return 0; if (n, W) in memo: return memo[(n, W)]; include = values[n-1] + knapsack(values, weights, n, W-weights[n-1], memo) if weights[n-1] <= W else 0; exclude = knapsack(values, weights, n-1, W, memo); memo[(n, W)] = max(include, exclude); return memo[(n, W)].

examples.trace: 1. Call knapsack(4,8). 2. Check base: n=4>0, W=8>0. 3. Not in memo. 4. Item4 weight=5 ≤8: include=70+knapsack(4,3). 5. knapsack(4,3): n=4,W=3; item4 weight=5>3: include=0; exclude=knapsack(3,3). 6. knapsack(3,3): n=3,W=3; item3 weight=4>3: include=0; exclude=knapsack(2,3). 7. knapsack(2,3): n=2,W=3; item2 weight=3≤3: include=40+knapsack(2,0). 8. knapsack(2,0): base return 0. include=40; exclude=knapsack(1,3). 9. knapsack(1,3): n=1,W=3; item1 weight=1≤3: include=10+knapsack(1,2). 10. knapsack(1,2): n=1,W=2; include=10+knapsack(1,1). 11. knapsack(1,1): n=1,W=1; include=10+knapsack(1,0)=10; exclude=0; max=10. 12. Back: knapsack(1,2)=max(20,0)=20; knapsack(1,3)=max(30,0)=30; knapsack(2,3)=max(40,30)=40; knapsack(3,3)=max(0,40)=40; knapsack(4,3)=max(0,40)=40; include for knapsack(4,8)=70+40=110; exclude=knapsack(3,8). 13. knapsack(3,8): n=3,W=8; item3 weight=4≤8: include=50+knapsack(3,4). 14. knapsack(3,4): n=3,W=4; item3 weight=4≤4: include=50+knapsack(3,0)=50; exclude=knapsack(2,4). 15. knapsack(2,4): n=2,W=4; item2 weight=3≤4: include=40+knapsack(2,1). 16. knapsack(2,1): n=2,W=1; item2 weight=3>1: include=0; exclude=knapsack(1,1)=10; max=10. include=40+10=50; exclude=knapsack(1,4). 17. knapsack(1,4): n=1,W=4; include=10+knapsack(1,3)=10+30=40; exclude=0; max=40. knapsack(2,4)=max(50,40)=50; knapsack(3,4)=max(50,50)=50; include for knapsack(3,8)=50+50=100; exclude=knapsack(2,8). 18. knapsack(2,8): n=2,W=8; item2 weight=3≤8: include=40+knapsack(2,5). 19. knapsack(2,5): n=2,W=5; include=40+knapsack(2,2). 20. knapsack(2,2): n=2,W=2; item2 weight=3>2: include=0; exclude=knapsack(1,2)=20; max=20. include=40+20=60; exclude=knapsack(1,5). 21. knapsack(1,5): n=1,W=5; include=10+knapsack(1,4)=10+40=50; exclude=0; max=50. knapsack(2,5)=max(60,50)=60; include for knapsack(2,8)=40+60=100; exclude=knapsack(1,8). 22. knapsack(1,8): n=1,W=8; include=10+knapsack(1,7)=10+70=80; knapsack(1,7)=10+knapsack(1,6)=10+60=70; knapsack(1,6)=10+knapsack(1,5)=10+50=60; knapsack(1,5)=50 as above; so knapsack(1,8)=80; exclude=0; max=80. knapsack(2,8)=max(100,80)=100; knapsack(3,8)=max(100,100)=100; exclude for knapsack(4,8)=100; max(110,100)=110. Return 110.

1. Call knapsack(3,3). 2. Base: n=3>0, W=3>0. 3. Not in memo. 4. Item3 weight=6>3: include=0; exclude=knapsack(2,3). 5. knapsack(2,3): n=2,W=3; item2 weight=5>3: include=0; exclude=knapsack(1,3). 6. knapsack(1,3): n=1,W=3; item1 weight=4>3: include=0; exclude=knapsack(0,3). 7. knapsack(0,3): base n=0 return 0. 8. Back: knapsack(1,3)=max(0,0)=0; knapsack(2,3)=max(0,0)=0; knapsack(3,3)=max(0,0)=0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes maximum value for given capacity and item index, using memoization to cache results.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that solves the unbounded knapsack subproblem. The intuition is to define a function f(i, w) that returns the maximum value achievable using first i items (1-indexed) with capacity w. It uses memoization to store computed results, avoiding recomputation. The algorithm: base cases: if w ≤ 0 or i == 0, return 0. Check memo dictionary for (i, w); if present, return cached value. Otherwise, if weight of item i-1 ≤ w, compute include = value[i-1] + f(i, w - weight[i-1]) (allowing reuse of item i). Compute exclude = f(i-1, w). Set result = max(include, exclude), store in memo, and return result. Time complexity for this function call is O(1) per state, but overall O(n*W) across all calls. Space is O(n*W) for memo. This feeds into L0 by being the main recursive engine. Common mistakes: incorrect base cases (e.g., not handling w=0), wrong memo key (using i instead of i-1), or not allowing item reuse in include case. Real code pattern: def solve(i, w, values, weights, memo): if w <= 0 or i == 0: return 0; if (i, w) in memo: return memo[(i, w)]; include = values[i-1] + solve(i, w-weights[i-1], values, weights, memo) if weights[i-1] <= w else 0; exclude = solve(i-1, w, values, weights, memo); memo[(i, w)] = max(include, exclude); return memo[(i, w)].

examples.trace: 1. Call solve(2,4). 2. Base: i=2>0, w=4>0. 3. Not in memo. 4. Item2 weight=3≤4: include=40+solve(2,1). 5. solve(2,1): i=2,w=1; item2 weight=3>1: include=0; exclude=solve(1,1). 6. solve(1,1): i=1,w=1; item1 weight=1≤1: include=10+solve(1,0). 7. solve(1,0): base w=0 return 0. include=10; exclude=solve(0,1)=0; max=10; memo[(1,1)]=10. 8. Back: solve(2,1)=max(0,10)=10; memo[(2,1)]=10. include for solve(2,4)=40+10=50. 9. exclude=solve(1,4). 10. solve(1,4): i=1,w=4; include=10+solve(1,3). 11. solve(1,3): i=1,w=3; include=10+solve(1,2). 12. solve(1,2): i=1,w=2; include=10+solve(1,1)=10+10=20; exclude=0; max=20; memo[(1,2)]=20. 13. Back: solve(1,3)=max(30,0)=30; memo[(1,3)]=30. solve(1,4)=max(40,0)=40; memo[(1,4)]=40. 14. exclude for solve(2,4)=40. max(50,40)=50; memo[(2,4)]=50. Return 50.
1. Call solve(1,1). 2. Base: i=1>0, w=1>0. 3. Not in memo. 4. Item1 weight=2>1: include=0; exclude=solve(0,1). 5. solve(0,1): base i=0 return 0. 6. max(0,0)=0; memo[(1,1)]=0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decision to Include or Exclude an Item with Reuse
ladders.desc: The central decision-making step: for each item, choose to include it (allowing reuse) or exclude it to maximize value.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the decision logic at each recursive step. The intuition is that for a given item and remaining capacity, we have two choices: include the item (add its value, reduce capacity by its weight, and keep the item available for reuse) or exclude it (move to the next item without using it). The maximum value is the better of these two options. This captures the unbounded nature: including an item doesn't remove it from consideration. The algorithm step: if item weight ≤ remaining capacity, compute include_value = item_value + recursive_call(same_item_index, capacity - item_weight). Compute exclude_value = recursive_call(next_item_index, capacity). Return max(include_value, exclude_value). Time per decision is O(1), but it drives the O(n*W) overall complexity. Space is O(1) per call. This feeds into L1 by being the recursive body. Common mistakes: forgetting to allow reuse (calling with same item index in include case), incorrect weight comparison, or not handling the exclude case. Real code pattern: if weights[i] <= w: include = values[i] + solve(i, w-weights[i]); exclude = solve(i-1, w); return max(include, exclude) else: return solve(i-1, w).

examples.input: item_value = 40, item_weight = 3, i = 2, w = 5, next_call results: solve(2,2)=10, solve(1,5)=40
examples.trace: 1. Check: item_weight=3 ≤ w=5? Yes. 2. Compute include = 40 + solve(2, 5-3=2). Given solve(2,2)=10, so include = 40+10=50. 3. Compute exclude = solve(1,5). Given solve(1,5)=40. 4. Return max(50,40)=50.

examples.input: item_value = 10, item_weight = 5, i = 1, w = 3, next_call results: solve(1,-2)=0, solve(0,3)=0
examples.trace: 1. Check: item_weight=5 ≤ w=3? No. 2. Since condition false, include not computed. 3. Return exclude = solve(0,3)=0.

testCases.input: item_value = 40, item_weight = 3, i = 2, w = 5, solve(2,2)=10, solve(1,5)=40
item_value = 10, item_weight = 5, i = 1, w = 3, solve(1,-2)=0, solve(0,3)=0
item_value = 7, item_weight = 2, i = 1, w = 6, solve(1,4)=14, solve(0,6)=0
item_value = 5, item_weight = 1, i = 1, w = 4, solve(1,3)=15, solve(0,4)=0
item_value = 12, item_weight = 4, i = 2, w = 7, solve(2,3)=6, solve(1,7)=18
item_value = 3, item_weight = 2, i = 1, w = 1, solve(1,-1)=0, solve(0,1)=0
item_value = 8, item_weight = 3, i = 2, w = 9, solve(2,6)=16, solve(1,9)=24
item_value = 6, item_weight = 3, i = 1, w = 6, solve(1,3)=6, solve(0,6)=0
item_value = 9, item_weight = 5, i = 1, w = 10, solve(1,5)=9, solve(0,10)=0
item_value = 2, item_weight = 1, i = 1, w = 0, solve(1,-1)=0, solve(0,0)=0

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization Dictionary Implementation
ladders.desc: Create and use a dictionary to cache results of subproblems, keyed by (item index, capacity).
ladders.explanation: Memoization is a technique to store results of expensive function calls and return cached results when the same inputs occur again. The intuition is to avoid redundant calculations in recursion by saving computed values. For the knapsack problem, each subproblem is defined by the current item index i and remaining capacity w. We use a dictionary where keys are tuples (i, w) and values are the maximum value for that state. The algorithm: before computing, check if (i, w) is in the dictionary; if yes, return the cached value. After computing the result, store it in the dictionary. This reduces time complexity from exponential to O(n*W). Space complexity is O(n*W) for the dictionary. This feeds into L2 by enabling efficient recursion. Common mistakes: using mutable keys incorrectly, not initializing the dictionary, or forgetting to store results. Real code pattern: memo = {}; def solve(i, w): if (i, w) in memo: return memo[(i, w)]; # compute result; memo[(i, w)] = result; return result.

examples.input: Compute solve(2,3) with values=[10,20], weights=[1,2], memo initially empty, then compute solve(2,3) again.
examples.trace: First call: 1. Check memo for (2,3): not present. 2. Compute: item2 weight=2≤3: include=20+solve(2,1). 3. solve(2,1): check memo (2,1): not present. Compute: item2 weight=2>1: include=0; exclude=solve(1,1). 4. solve(1,1): check memo (1,1): not present. Compute: item1 weight=1≤1: include=10+solve(1,0). 5. solve(1,0): base return 0. include=10; exclude=solve(0,1)=0; result=10; store memo[(1,1)]=10. 6. Back: solve(2,1)=max(0,10)=10; store memo[(2,1)]=10. include for solve(2,3)=20+10=30. 7. exclude=solve(1,3). 8. solve(1,3): check memo (1,3): not present. Compute: include=10+solve(1,2). 9. solve(1,2): check memo (1,2): not present. Compute: include=10+solve(1,1)=10+10=20; exclude=0; result=20; store memo[(1,2)]=20. 10. solve(1,3)=max(30,0)=30; store memo[(1,3)]=30. exclude=30. max(30,30)=30; store memo[(2,3)]=30. Return 30. Second call: 1. Check memo for (2,3): present with value 30. 2. Return 30 directly without recomputation.

examples.input: Compute solve(1,1) with values=[5], weights=[3], memo empty, then solve(1,1) again.
examples.trace: First call: 1. Check memo (1,1): not present. 2. Compute: item1 weight=3>1: include=0; exclude=solve(0,1)=0; result=0; store memo[(1,1)]=0. Return 0. Second call: 1. Check memo (1,1): present value 0. 2. Return 0.

testCases.input: memo = {}, key = (2,5), value = 50, store and retrieve
memo = {}, key = (0,0), value = 0, store and retrieve

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function Call with Parameters
ladders.desc: Make a recursive function call, passing current state (item index and capacity) as parameters.
ladders.explanation: This level involves the fundamental operation of calling a function recursively with updated parameters. The intuition is to break a problem into smaller subproblems by invoking the same function with reduced inputs. For knapsack, we call the function with either reduced capacity (if including an item) or reduced item index (if excluding). The algorithm: define a function solve(i, w) that takes current item index i and remaining capacity w. In the recursive step, call solve(i, w - weight) for include case and solve(i-1, w) for exclude case. Base cases stop recursion when i=0 or w≤0. Time per call is O(1), but total calls depend on problem size. Space includes recursion stack O(W). This feeds into L3 by being the mechanism that memoization optimizes. Common mistakes: infinite recursion due to missing base cases, incorrect parameter updates, or stack overflow for large W. Real code pattern: def solve(i, w): if i == 0 or w <= 0: return 0; return max(values[i-1] + solve(i, w-weights[i-1]), solve(i-1, w)) if weights[i-1] <= w else solve(i-1, w).

examples.input: Call solve(2,4) where solve(2,1) returns 10 and solve(1,4) returns 40, with item_weight=3, item_value=40.
examples.trace: 1. Check base: i=2>0, w=4>0. 2. Check item_weight=3 ≤4? Yes. 3. Compute include = 40 + solve(2, 4-3=1). Given solve(2,1)=10, so include=50. 4. Compute exclude = solve(1,4). Given solve(1,4)=40. 5. Return max(50,40)=50.

examples.input: Call solve(1,2) where solve(1,1) returns 5 and solve(0,2) returns 0, with item_weight=1, item_value=5.
examples.trace: 1. Base: i=1>0, w=2>0. 2. item_weight=1 ≤2? Yes. 3. Include = 5 + solve(1, 2-1=1). Given solve(1,1)=5, so include=10. 4. Exclude = solve(0,2)=0. 5. Return max(10,0)=10.

testCases.input: solve(2,5) with solve(2,2)=10, solve(1,5)=20, item_weight=3, item_value=15
solve(1,3) with solve(1,2)=4, solve(0,3)=0, item_weight=1, item_value=2
solve(3,6) with solve(3,3)=9, solve(2,6)=12, item_weight=3, item_value=5
solve(2,4) with solve(2,1)=3, solve(1,4)=8, item_weight=3, item_value=7
solve(1,1) with solve(1,0)=0, solve(0,1)=0, item_weight=2, item_value=3
solve(2,7) with solve(2,4)=10, solve(1,7)=14, item_weight=3, item_value=6
solve(1,5) with solve(1,4)=8, solve(0,5)=0, item_weight=1, item_value=2
solve(3,8) with solve(3,5)=11, solve(2,8)=16, item_weight=3, item_value=4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Function Definition and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Define a recursive function with parameters and base cases to stop recursion.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept of writing a recursive function. The intuition is to solve a problem by defining a function that calls itself with smaller inputs, eventually reaching a base case where the result is known directly. For knapsack, the function takes parameters for item index and capacity. Base cases are conditions where recursion stops: if capacity is 0 or negative (no more space), return 0; if item index is 0 (no items left), return 0. This prevents infinite recursion. The algorithm: define function solve(i, w): if i == 0 or w <= 0: return 0; otherwise, proceed with recursive calls. Time for base case is O(1). Space is O(1) per call. This feeds into L4 by providing the recursive structure. Common mistakes: missing base cases causing infinite recursion, incorrect base conditions (e.g., w < 0 instead of w <= 0), or not handling edge cases. Real code pattern: def solve(i, w): if i <= 0 or w <= 0: return 0; # rest of function.

examples.input: Define solve(i,w) with base cases, call solve(0,5).
examples.trace: 1. Function solve(i,w): if i == 0 or w <= 0: return 0. 2. Call solve(0,5). 3. Check condition: i=0, so return 0.

examples.input: Define solve(i,w) with base cases, call solve(3,0).
examples.trace: 1. Function solve(i,w): if i == 0 or w <= 0: return 0. 2. Call solve(3,0). 3. Check condition: w=0, so return 0.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Tabulation O(n*W)
approaches.summary: Build a DP table iteratively (bottom-up) where dp[w] stores the maximum value for capacity w, updating it by considering each item multiple times due to the unbounded nature. This eliminates recursion overhead and is often more space-efficient with optimizations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Unbounded Knapsack with DP Tabulation
ladders.desc: Complete solution for unbounded knapsack using bottom-up DP with O(W) space optimization.
ladders.explanation: The unbounded knapsack problem asks: given items with weights and values, and a knapsack capacity W, find the maximum total value achievable where each item can be taken unlimited times. The DP tabulation approach builds a 1D array dp where dp[w] stores the maximum value for capacity w. We initialize dp[0] = 0 (no capacity yields no value). For each capacity w from 1 to W, we iterate through all items: if an item's weight ≤ w, we consider taking it (adding its value to dp[w - weight]) and update dp[w] to the maximum of its current value and this new possibility. This works because dp[w - weight] already contains the optimal value for the remaining capacity after taking one copy of the item, and since items are unbounded, we can reuse this value. The key insight is that by processing capacities in increasing order, we allow multiple copies of the same item to be considered naturally—when we compute dp[w], dp[w - weight] might have already included the same item earlier in the loop. Time complexity is O(n*W) where n is number of items, as we have nested loops over items and capacities. Space complexity is O(W) since we only maintain a 1D array. This is more efficient than the 2D DP version which uses O(n*W) space. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key subroutine of updating dp[w] by considering all items, which is the core of the DP recurrence. Common mistakes include not initializing dp properly (should be zeros except dp[0]=0), or processing items in the wrong order (must iterate items inside capacity loop to allow unbounded use). Real code pattern: Initialize dp array of size W+1 with zeros. For w in 1 to W: For each item (weight, value): if weight ≤ w: dp[w] = max(dp[w], value + dp[w - weight]). Return dp[W].

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0..10] = zeros. w=1: All weights>1 skip, dp[1]=0. w=2: item0 (weight2, value3): 2≤2 → dp[2]=max(0,3+dp[0]=3)=3. Others skip. dp[2]=3. w=3: item0: 2≤3 → dp[3]=max(0,3+dp[1]=3)=3. item1 (weight3, value4): 3≤3 → dp[3]=max(3,4+dp[0]=4)=4. dp[3]=4. w=4: item0: 2≤4 → dp[4]=max(0,3+dp[2]=6)=6. item1: 3≤4 → dp[4]=max(6,4+dp[1]=4)=6. dp[4]=6. w=5: item0: 2≤5 → dp[5]=max(0,3+dp[3]=7)=7. item1: 3≤5 → dp[5]=max(7,4+dp[2]=7)=7. item2 (weight5, value6): 5≤5 → dp[5]=max(7,6+dp[0]=6)=7. dp[5]=7. w=6: item0: 2≤6 → dp[6]=max(0,3+dp[4]=9)=9. item1: 3≤6 → dp[6]=max(9,4+dp[3]=8)=9. item2: 5≤6 → dp[6]=max(9,6+dp[1]=6)=9. dp[6]=9. w=7: item0: 2≤7 → dp[7]=max(0,3+dp[5]=10)=10. item1: 3≤7 → dp[7]=max(10,4+dp[4]=10)=10. item2: 5≤7 → dp[7]=max(10,6+dp[2]=9)=10. dp[7]=10. w=8: item0: 2≤8 → dp[8]=max(0,3+dp[6]=12)=12. item1: 3≤8 → dp[8]=max(12,4+dp[5]=11)=12. item2: 5≤8 → dp[8]=max(12,6+dp[3]=10)=12. dp[8]=12. w=9: item0: 2≤9 → dp[9]=max(0,3+dp[7]=13)=13. item1: 3≤9 → dp[9]=max(13,4+dp[6]=13)=13. item2: 5≤9 → dp[9]=max(13,6+dp[4]=12)=13. dp[9]=13. w=10: item0: 2≤10 → dp[10]=max(0,3+dp[8]=15)=15. item1: 3≤10 → dp[10]=max(15,4+dp[7]=14)=15. item2: 5≤10 → dp[10]=max(15,6+dp[5]=13)=15. But wait, also consider item0 again? Actually loop continues: after item2, we have dp[10]=15. However, check: For w=10, we can take item0 five times (5*3=15) or item1 three times and item0 twice (3*4+2*3=18)? Let's recompute carefully: At w=10, after item0: dp[10]=15. item1: 3≤10 → dp[10]=max(15,4+dp[7]=4+10=14)=15. item2: 5≤10 → dp[10]=max(15,6+dp[5]=6+7=13)=15. But is 15 optimal? Consider taking item1 three times (weight9, value12) and item0 once (weight2, value3) total weight11 exceeds capacity. Or item1 twice (weight6, value8) and item0 twice (weight4, value6) total weight10 value14. Or item2 twice (weight10, value12). Actually the optimal is item0 five times: weight10 value15. So dp[10]=15. Wait, but earlier I said output 16? Let's correct: The optimal is actually item1 three times (weight9 value12) and item0? No, that's weight11. Or item2 twice (weight10 value12). Or item0 four times (weight8 value12) and item1? weight8+3=11. Hmm, maybe I made an error. Let's trace properly: At w=10, dp[10] starts 0. item0: 2≤10 → dp[10]=max(0,3+dp[8]=3+12=15)=15. item1: 3≤10 → dp[10]=max(15,4+dp[7]=4+10=14)=15. item2: 5≤10 → dp[10]=max(15,6+dp[5]=6+7=13)=15. So dp[10]=15. But is there a combination giving 16? Let's brute: Items: (2,3), (3,4), (5,6). Capacity10. Try all: 5*item0: weight10 value15. 3*item1+1*item0: weight11 invalid. 2*item1+2*item0: weight10 value14. 2*item2: weight10 value12. 1*item2+2*item1: weight11 invalid. 1*item2+1*item1+1*item0: weight10 value13. So maximum is 15. Therefore output should be 15, not 16. I'll correct the output to 15.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Update for a Single Capacity
ladders.desc: Given current dp array and items, update dp[w] for a specific capacity w.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the unbounded knapsack DP solution. For a fixed capacity w, we need to compute the maximum value achievable by considering all items that can fit into this capacity. The intuition is that dp[w] should be the maximum over all items i where weight[i] ≤ w of (value[i] + dp[w - weight[i]]). Here, dp[w - weight[i]] represents the optimal value for the remaining capacity after taking one copy of item i. Since items are unbounded, dp[w - weight[i]] might already include item i itself, allowing multiple copies. The algorithm: Initialize maxVal = dp[w] (current value, which might be from previous iterations or initialization). For each item i from 0 to n-1: if weight[i] ≤ w, compute candidate = value[i] + dp[w - weight[i]]. If candidate > maxVal, update maxVal = candidate. After looping through all items, set dp[w] = maxVal. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) since we iterate through all items once. Space complexity is O(1) extra space beyond the dp array. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called for each capacity w from 1 to W in the full solution, building the dp array incrementally. Common mistakes include not considering all items (e.g., skipping some), or incorrectly accessing dp indices (ensure w - weight[i] is non-negative). Real code pattern: maxVal = dp[w]; for i in range(n): if weight[i] <= w: candidate = value[i] + dp[w - weight[i]]; maxVal = max(maxVal, candidate); dp[w] = maxVal.

examples.trace: Start with maxVal = dp[8] = 100. Check item0: weight1 ≤8 → candidate = value10 + dp[7] = 10 + 90 = 100. maxVal = max(100,100)=100. Item1: weight3 ≤8 → candidate = value40 + dp[5] = 40 + 70 = 110. maxVal = max(100,110)=110. Item2: weight4 ≤8 → candidate = value50 + dp[4] = 50 + 50 = 100. maxVal = max(110,100)=110. Item3: weight5 ≤8 → candidate = value70 + dp[3] = 70 + 40 = 110. maxVal = max(110,110)=110. Set dp[8] = 110.

Start with maxVal = dp[10] = 15. Check item0: weight2 ≤10 → candidate = value3 + dp[8] = 3 + 12 = 15. maxVal = max(15,15)=15. Item1: weight3 ≤10 → candidate = value4 + dp[7] = 4 + 10 = 14. maxVal = max(15,14)=15. Item2: weight5 ≤10 → candidate = value6 + dp[5] = 6 + 7 = 13. maxVal = max(15,13)=15. Set dp[10] = 15.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence for Unbounded Knapsack
ladders.desc: Derive and understand the recurrence relation: dp[w] = max over i (value[i] + dp[w - weight[i]]) for weight[i] ≤ w.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the unbounded knapsack problem is the recurrence relation that defines the optimal substructure. For a given capacity w, the maximum value dp[w] can be achieved by either taking no item (which gives 0, but we consider items) or by taking one copy of some item i and then optimally filling the remaining capacity w - weight[i]. Since items are unbounded, after taking item i, we can still take more copies of any item, including i itself, to fill the remaining capacity. Therefore, dp[w] = max_{i: weight[i] ≤ w} (value[i] + dp[w - weight[i]]). The base case is dp[0] = 0, as with zero capacity, we can take no items. The intuition is that we break the problem into smaller subproblems: to solve for capacity w, we look at all possible first items we could take, and then rely on the solution for the smaller capacity w - weight[i]. This works because the problem has optimal substructure—the optimal solution for w contains optimal solutions for smaller capacities. The recurrence is similar to the 0/1 knapsack but without the restriction of taking each item only once; here, we can reuse items, which is reflected by the fact that dp[w - weight[i]] might include item i itself. Time analysis for computing dp[w] using this recurrence is O(n) if we check all items. Space is O(W) for storing dp array. Connection to L1: This recurrence is implemented in the key sub-routine by iterating through items and updating dp[w]. Common mistakes include using the wrong recurrence (e.g., dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w - weight[i]] + value[i]) but forgetting to iterate items), or not handling base cases. Real code pattern: dp[0] = 0; for w in 1..W: dp[w] = 0; for i in 0..n-1: if weight[i] <= w: dp[w] = max(dp[w], value[i] + dp[w - weight[i]]).

examples.trace: Base: dp[0]=0. For w=1: Check item0: weight1≤1 → candidate=10+dp[0]=10. Item1: weight3>1 skip. So dp[1]=10. w=2: item0: weight1≤2 → candidate=10+dp[1]=20. Item1 skip. dp[2]=20. w=3: item0: weight1≤3 → candidate=10+dp[2]=30. Item1: weight3≤3 → candidate=40+dp[0]=40. max(30,40)=40. So dp[3]=40.

dp[0]=0. w=1: both weights>1, dp[1]=0. w=2: item0: weight2≤2 → candidate=3+dp[0]=3. Item1 skip. dp[2]=3. w=3: item0: weight2≤3 → candidate=3+dp[1]=3. Item1: weight3≤3 → candidate=4+dp[0]=4. max(3,4)=4. dp[3]=4. w=4: item0: weight2≤4 → candidate=3+dp[2]=6. Item1: weight3≤4 → candidate=4+dp[1]=4. max(6,4)=6. dp[4]=6. w=5: item0: weight2≤5 → candidate=3+dp[3]=7. Item1: weight3≤5 → candidate=4+dp[2]=7. max(7,7)=7. dp[5]=7.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Items for a Given Capacity
ladders.desc: Given a capacity w and list of items, find all items that can fit and compute potential values.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the process of scanning through all items to determine which ones can be included in the knapsack for a specific capacity w. For each item, we check if its weight is less than or equal to w. If it is, we calculate the value that would be obtained by taking that item once, which is simply the item's value. However, in the context of unbounded knapsack, we later combine this with the DP value for the remaining capacity. The intuition is to identify feasible items and gather data for further computation. The algorithm: Initialize an empty list or variables to track items. For each item i from 0 to n-1: if weight[i] ≤ w, record the item's index, weight, and value. This step is O(n) time and O(1) space if just counting, or O(n) space if storing all feasible items. Connection to L2: This block is part of the recurrence where we iterate items to compute max(value[i] + dp[w - weight[i]]). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, or incorrect comparison (using < instead of ≤). Real code pattern: feasible = []; for i in range(n): if weight[i] <= w: feasible.append((weight[i], value[i])).

examples.trace: Check item0: weight1 ≤8 → feasible. Item1: weight3 ≤8 → feasible. Item2: weight4 ≤8 → feasible. Item3: weight5 ≤8 → feasible. All items are feasible.
Check item0: weight2 ≤4 → feasible. Item1: weight3 ≤4 → feasible. Item2: weight5 >4 → not feasible. So feasible items are item0 and item1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if an Item Fits in Capacity
ladders.desc: Given an item's weight and a capacity, determine if the item can be included.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation in knapsack problems: comparing an item's weight to the available capacity to see if it can fit. The intuition is simple: an item can only be taken if its weight does not exceed the remaining capacity of the knapsack. Mathematically, we check if weight ≤ capacity. If true, the item is feasible; if false, it cannot be included. This operation is performed repeatedly in knapsack algorithms for each item and capacity combination. Time complexity is O(1), as it's a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used within the loop that iterates over items to build the list of feasible items. Common mistakes include using strict inequality (<) instead of ≤ (since weight equal to capacity means the item exactly fits), or comparing value instead of weight. Real code pattern: if weight <= capacity: return True else return False.

examples.trace: 3 ≤ 5 is true, so item fits.
7 ≤ 5 is false, so item does not fit.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Weight and Capacity
ladders.desc: Learn the basic concepts of weight (item property) and capacity (knapsack limit).
ladders.explanation: In knapsack problems, each item has a weight, which represents how much space or resource it consumes. The knapsack has a capacity, which is the maximum total weight it can hold. The goal is to select items such that their total weight does not exceed the capacity, while maximizing total value. Weight and capacity are non-negative numbers, typically integers. The intuition is similar to packing a bag: you have items with different sizes (weights) and a bag with a size limit (capacity), and you want to pack as much value as possible without overfilling. This foundation is crucial because all knapsack variants build upon this constraint. Connection to L4: This concept is used when checking if an item fits by comparing its weight to the capacity. Common mistakes include confusing weight with value, or not understanding that capacity is a limit on total weight, not on number of items. Real code pattern: items are represented as pairs (weight, value); capacity is a single number.

examples.input: Item: weight = 2 kg, value = $5. Knapsack capacity = 10 kg.
examples.output: The item weighs 2 kg, which is less than 10 kg, so it can fit.
examples.trace: Weight 2 is compared to capacity 10: 2 ≤ 10, so yes, it fits.

examples.input: Item: weight = 12 kg, value = $20. Knapsack capacity = 10 kg.
examples.output: The item weighs 12 kg, which is more than 10 kg, so it cannot fit.
examples.trace: Weight 12 is compared to capacity 10: 12 ≤ 10 is false, so it does not fit.
